On decode, LastWill::read calls read_mqtt_bytes then explicitly validates with validate_mqtt_string (UTF-8 + no U+0000). On encode, LastWill::write calls validate_mqtt_string on the Bytes-backed topic before write_mqtt_bytes. The explicit validation on both paths is correct for a Bytes-backed field (unlike v4 where topic is String).

authoritative_source.summary: If the Will Flag is set to 1, the Will Topic is the next field in the Payload. The Will Topic MUST be a UTF-8 Encoded String as defined in section 1.5.4 [MQTT-3.1.3-11].

mapping_review.notes: The original entry correctly maps the authoritative requirement. The summary matches the v5 spec text exactly.

evidence.reason: LastWill::read decodes will_topic via read_mqtt_bytes then validates with validate_mqtt_string (enforces well-formed UTF-8 + no U+0000). LastWill::write validates with validate_mqtt_string before write_mqtt_bytes. Both paths explicitly validate because LastWill.topic is Bytes, not String.
v5 validate_mqtt_string delegates to core_primitives::validate_mqtt_string, which uses std::str::from_utf8 (rejects ill-formed UTF-8 including surrogates) and checks for U+0000 bytes.
Core primitive enforces well-formed UTF-8 via std::str::from_utf8 and rejects U+0000.

test_coverage.notes: Symmetric coverage: both encode and decode paths tested for both U+0000 and malformed UTF-8 rejection at the Will Topic integration level.

requirements.summary: Both strings in a UTF-8 String Pair MUST comply with the requirements for UTF-8 Encoded Strings. If a receiver receives a string pair which does not meet these requirements it is a Malformed Packet.
requirements.mapping_reason: Both halves of every UserProperty string pair are validated through shared read_mqtt_string (decode) and write_mqtt_string (encode) helpers, which enforce UTF-8 well-formedness, surrogate rejection, and U+0000 rejection. Decode failures map to DisconnectReasonCode::MalformedPacket in framed.rs.

authoritative_source.summary: Both strings in a UTF-8 String Pair MUST comply with UTF-8 Encoded String requirements (MQTT-1.5.4-1, MQTT-1.5.4-2, MQTT-1.5.4-3). A receiver that gets a non-conforming string pair MUST treat it as a Malformed Packet.

mapping_review.notes: The generated entry's summary accurately captures both the outbound UTF-8 validation obligation and the receiver-side Malformed Packet consequence from the authoritative text.

evidence.reason: Shared primitive validates each string via std::str::from_utf8 (rejects surrogates, overlong, truncated sequences) and rejects U+0000. Used for both key and value in every string pair read path.
v5 write_mqtt_string calls validate_mqtt_string before serializing, preventing outbound string pairs with ill-formed UTF-8 or U+0000.
CONNECT and Will UserProperty pairs use two consecutive read_mqtt_string/write_mqtt_string calls, each independently validating both key and value.
PUBLISH UserProperty pairs validated on both read and write paths.
SUBSCRIBE UserProperty pairs validated on both read and write paths. Also contains dedicated malformed string pair tests.
Maps TopicNotUtf8 and MalformedPacket decode errors to DisconnectReasonCode::MalformedPacket (0x81), satisfying the receiver-side Malformed Packet obligation.

test_coverage.notes: Tests in subscribe.rs prove both key and value are validated on decode for invalid UTF-8 and U+0000. Encode-side null rejection also tested. The shared primitives have additional surrogate and malformed UTF-8 tests in mqttbytes-core.

requirements.summary: The Property Length is encoded as a Variable Byte Integer. The Property Length does not include the bytes used to encode itself, but includes the length of the Properties. If there are no properties, this MUST be indicated by including a Property Length of zero.
requirements.mapping_reason: All property-bearing v5 packet types encode Property Length via write_remaining_length (VBI), compute the length value as the property data byte count excluding the VBI encoding itself (properties_len = p.len(), with len_len(properties_len) tracked separately for Remaining Length), and emit write_remaining_length(buffer, 0) when no properties are present. Decoders read the VBI with length(), advance past it, and return Ok(None) when properties_len == 0.

authoritative_source.summary: The Property Length is encoded as a Variable Byte Integer. The Property Length does not include the bytes used to encode itself, but includes the length of the Properties. If there are no properties, this MUST be indicated by including a Property Length of zero.

mapping_review.notes: The original summary is a near-verbatim copy of the authoritative spec text (verified verbatim against docs/spec/mqtt-v5.0.md section 2.2.2.1, line 1344, including the [MQTT-2.2.2-1] marker on the 'Property Length of zero' MUST clause). The mapping correctly identifies the three normative obligations: VBI encoding, self-exclusion of the VBI's own bytes, and zero-indication. Re-audit on 2026-06-22 re-verified the v5 codec paths: Publish::len()/write()/PublishProperties::read() in rumqttc-v5/src/mqttbytes/v5/publish.rs and write_remaining_length()/length() in mqttbytes-core/src/primitives.rs all enforce the obligations, and the four named tests in v5/mod.rs and primitives.rs still pass byte-level assertions covering all 13 property-bearing packet types plus multi-byte VBI.

evidence.reason: write_remaining_length encodes a Variable Byte Integer; length() decodes one. These are the shared primitives used for Property Length encoding and decoding across all v5 packet types.

Publish::write calls write_remaining_length(buffer, 0) when properties are None. PublishProperties::write calls write_remaining_length(buffer, self.len()) where len() returns the property data byte count. PublishProperties::read decodes the VBI with length() and returns Ok(None) when properties_len == 0.

CONNECT, Will Properties, and their property structs all encode Property Length with write_remaining_length (including 0 for absent properties) and decode with length(). The length value excludes VBI encoding bytes (len_len tracked separately in parent len()).
CONNACK properties encode Property Length via write_remaining_length and decode via length(), returning Ok(None) on zero.
PUBACK properties encode/decode Property Length with write_remaining_length/length, handling zero-property case.
PUBREC properties follow the same VBI property length encode/decode pattern.
PUBREL properties follow the same VBI property length encode/decode pattern.
PUBCOMP properties follow the same VBI property length encode/decode pattern.
SUBSCRIBE properties follow the same VBI property length encode/decode pattern.
SUBACK properties follow the same VBI property length encode/decode pattern.
UNSUBSCRIBE properties follow the same VBI property length encode/decode pattern.
UNSUBACK properties follow the same VBI property length encode/decode pattern.
DISCONNECT properties encode Property Length with write_remaining_length and decode with length(), handling zero.
AUTH properties encode Property Length via write_remaining_length and decode via length(), returning Ok(None) on zero.

test_coverage.notes: The dedicated zero-property-length test covers all 13 packet types with properties: None, verifying each emits 0x00 (Property Length = 0) and round-trips correctly. The non-zero test verifies a 5-byte property section encodes as a single-byte VBI (0x05) excluding its own encoding bytes, with byte-level inspection and round-trip decode. The multi-byte VBI test uses 148 bytes of property data (4 user properties + session_expiry + receive_maximum) to verify 2-byte VBI encoding with the continuation bit set and correct decoded value. The VBI primitive round-trip test covers 0, 127, 128, 321, 16384, and 268435455.

requirements.summary: The Server MUST validate that the CONNECT packet matches the format described in section 3.1 and close the Network Connection if it does not match. The Server MAY send a CONNACK with a Reason Code of 0x80 or greater as described in section 4.13 before closing the Network Connection.
requirements.mapping_reason: Authoritative text (section 3.1.4, item 2) is a Server-only obligation: the server that receives a CONNECT must validate that it matches the section 3.1 format and, on mismatch, close the Network Connection (optionally sending a CONNACK with a Reason Code >= 0x80 first). A rumqttc-v5 client never acts as the CONNECT receiver (it only sends CONNECT and consumes the resulting CONNACK), so the normative validation/rejection obligation is outside the client's role. The client's own outbound CONNECT validation in Connect::write and ConnectProperties::write is the client's conformance to the separate section 3.1.2/3.1.3 packet-format requirements, not this server obligation. This is the direct v5 analogue of the v3.1.1 MQTT-3.1.4-1; the only substantive difference is that v5 permits (but does not require) the server to send an error CONNACK before closing, whereas v3.1.1 says to close without sending a CONNACK. Neither variant imposes any client-side obligation.

authoritative_source.summary: The Server MUST validate that the CONNECT packet matches the format described in section 3.1 and close the Network Connection if it does not match. The Server MAY send a CONNACK with a Reason Code of 0x80 or greater as described in section 4.13 before closing the Network Connection.

mapping_review.notes: The generated v5 requirements.json entry maps the authoritative server-only obligation correctly. The summary is faithful but truncated with '...'; the truncated portion is the MAY clause about sending an error CONNACK, which is fully captured by the authoritative_source.summary in this entry. Verified against docs/spec/mqtt-v5.0.md section 3.1.4 item 2 (line 2310) and the normative-summary table (line 6718). The actor is explicitly 'The Server'. As confirmed by the user, no v5 fix-attempt file exists, so fix_attempt_* are recorded as 'absent'. The not_applicable classification is justified because rumqttc-v5 is a client and never receives CONNECT packets in its normal protocol role.

evidence.reason: The client only constructs/sends its own outbound CONNECT (Connect::write serializes fixed header, protocol name 'MQTT', protocol level 0x05, connect flags, keep-alive, properties, client id, will, auth). Any validation here is the client's own conformance to section 3.1.2/3.1.3 packet-format requirements, not enforcement of the server-only MQTT-3.1.4-1 validation obligation.

The event loop is the CONNECT sender: it builds the outgoing Connect and then awaits the server's CONNACK. It never acts as the CONNECT receiver, confirming the server-only validation obligation is outside the client's role.

Defensive handling maps an inbound Connect to StateError::ProtocolViolation(UnexpectedIncomingPacket(PacketType::Connect)) via the catch-all '_' branch. This is the client's reaction to a server that misbehaves by sending CONNECT, not enforcement of the server's own validation/rejection obligation in MQTT-3.1.4-1.

test_coverage.notes: No client-side test is required for the server-only MQTT-3.1.4-1 validation obligation. The referenced state.rs test documents the client's defensive reaction to an unexpected inbound CONNECT, which is a separate concern.

requirements.summary: If the Keep Alive value is non-zero and the Server does not receive an MQTT Control Packet from the Client within one and a half times the Keep Alive time period, it MUST close the Network Connection to the Client as if the network had failed.
requirements.mapping_reason: Server MUST close the Network Connection if Keep Alive is non-zero and it does not receive an MQTT Control Packet from the Client within 1.5 times the Keep Alive period. This exact requirement is server-side timeout enforcement. Client PINGREQ and Server Keep Alive handling are adjacent client obligations covered by separate requirement IDs.

authoritative_source.summary: If the Keep Alive value is non-zero and the Server does not receive an MQTT Control Packet from the Client within one and a half times the Keep Alive time period, it MUST close the Network Connection to the Client as if the network had failed.

mapping_review.notes: The generated entry correctly identified this as a server-only obligation and marked it not_applicable. The mapping_reason accurately distinguishes this server-side timeout from the client-side PINGREQ obligation in MQTT-3.1.2-20.

evidence.reason: The authoritative text explicitly assigns the MUST obligation to 'the Server' (it MUST close the Network Connection). The preceding sentences in the same section assign the client's PINGREQ obligation to [MQTT-3.1.2-20] and the Server Keep Alive override to [MQTT-3.1.2-21], confirming that this specific tag covers only the server-side timeout behavior.

test_coverage.notes: No rumqttc client test coverage is required for a strictly server-exclusive MQTT Server obligation. The client's complementary PINGREQ behavior is tested under MQTT-3.1.2-20.

requirements.summary: Where a Packet is too large to send, the Server MUST discard it without sending it and then behave as if it had completed sending that Application Message.
requirements.mapping_reason: Server MUST discard an Application Message without sending it when the packet would be too large to send, and then behave as if sending had completed. This exact requirement is server-side behavior. Client-side Maximum Packet Size enforcement and oversized inbound packet handling are adjacent obligations covered separately.

authoritative_source.summary: Where a Packet is too large to send, the Server MUST discard it without sending it and then behave as if it had completed sending that Application Message.

mapping_review.notes: The generated entry correctly identified this as a server-only obligation and marked it not_applicable. The mapping_reason accurately distinguishes the server's discard obligation from the client's outgoing packet size enforcement.

evidence.reason: The authoritative text explicitly assigns the MUST obligation to 'the Server' (MUST discard it without sending it). The preceding requirement [MQTT-3.1.2-24] covers the server's MUST NOT send exceeding Maximum Packet Size and the client's DISCONNECT reaction. This specific tag [MQTT-3.1.2-25] covers only the server-side discard-and-behave-as-if-completed obligation.

test_coverage.notes: No rumqttc client test coverage is required for a strictly server-exclusive MQTT Server obligation. The client's outgoing max-packet-size enforcement is tested separately.

requirements.summary: If the ClientID represents a Client already connected to the Server, the Server sends a DISCONNECT packet to the existing Client with Reason Code of 0x8E (Session taken over) and MUST close the Network Connection of the existing Client.
requirements.mapping_reason: Server-side. If the ClientID represents an already connected Client, the Server sends DISCONNECT with Reason Code 0x8E (Session taken over) to the existing Client and MUST close that existing Network Connection. rumqttc-v5 may receive and surface that DISCONNECT as a client, but the takeover decision and forced close are server obligations.

authoritative_source.summary: If the ClientID represents a Client already connected to the Server, the Server sends a DISCONNECT packet to the existing Client with Reason Code of 0x8E (Session taken over) as described in section 4.13 and MUST close the Network Connection of the existing Client.

mapping_review.notes: The original entry correctly identified this as server-only and marked not_applicable. The summary is faithful to the authoritative text. The client defensively decodes incoming SessionTakenOver DISCONNECT packets, but that is separate defensive behavior, not this requirement.

evidence.reason: The authoritative text explicitly assigns the MUST obligation to 'the Server' (the Server sends DISCONNECT and MUST close the Network Connection). The client has no obligation to validate, reject, encode, decode, store, retransmit, reconcile, or react to this server behavior beyond normal DISCONNECT handling.

test_coverage.notes: No rumqttc client test coverage is required for a strictly server-exclusive MQTT Server obligation.

requirements.summary: The Server MUST send a CONNACK with a 0x00 (Success) Reason Code before sending any Packet other than AUTH.
requirements.mapping_reason: Server-side. The Server MUST send a CONNACK with 0x00 (Success) before sending any Packet other than AUTH. rumqttc-v5 does not send CONNACK packets. Client-side handling of CONNACK/AUTH ordering belongs under separate receive/handshake behavior.

authoritative_source.summary: The CONNACK packet is the packet sent by the Server in response to a CONNECT packet received from a Client. The Server MUST send a CONNACK with a 0x00 (Success) Reason Code before sending any Packet other than AUTH.

mapping_review.notes: The original entry correctly identified this as server-only and marked not_applicable. The summary captures the CONNACK ordering obligation. The client defensively handles the observable server behavior (accepting only AUTH before CONNACK), but that is separate defensive behavior, not this requirement.

evidence.reason: The authoritative text explicitly assigns the MUST obligation to 'the Server' (the Server MUST send a CONNACK with a 0x00 Reason Code before sending any Packet other than AUTH). The client has no obligation to validate, reject, encode, decode, store, retransmit, reconcile, or react to this server packet-ordering behavior beyond normal CONNACK handling.

requirements.summary: When the Server receives a SUBSCRIBE packet from a Client, the Server MUST respond with a SUBACK packet.
requirements.mapping_reason: Server-side. When the Server receives SUBSCRIBE from a Client, the Server MUST respond with SUBACK. rumqttc-v5 sends SUBSCRIBE and receives/handles SUBACK, but this exact requirement governs the server's response obligation.

authoritative_source.summary: When the Server receives a SUBSCRIBE packet from a Client, the Server MUST respond with a SUBACK packet.

mapping_review.notes: The original entry merged MQTT-3.8.4-1 with MQTT-3.8.4-2 (the SUBACK Packet Identifier correlation rule). The combined summary is not a faithful representation of the individual requirement. The compliance_status (not_applicable) is correct since the obligation is server-only. The client tracks pending SUBSCRIBE/SUBACK correlation, but that is separate behavior covered by MQTT-3.8.4-2.

evidence.reason: The authoritative text explicitly assigns the MUST obligation to 'the Server' (the Server MUST respond with a SUBACK packet). The client has no obligation to validate, reject, encode, decode, store, retransmit, reconcile, or react to this server response behavior beyond normal SUBACK handling.

requirements.summary: The Server MUST validate that the reserved flag in the CONNECT packet is set to 0. If the reserved flag is not 0 it is a Malformed Packet.
requirements.mapping_reason: The authoritative text places the MUST on the Server: 'The Server MUST validate that the reserved flag in the CONNECT packet is set to 0 ... it is a Malformed Packet.' That validation/Malformed-Packet handling obligation is server-only and cannot be implemented by this client crate, so the requirement is not_applicable for rumqttc-v5. As defensive/client-adjacent behavior, the v5 codec correctly emits bit 0 = 0 on encode (Connect::write starts connect_flags at 0 and only ORs in defined bits) and rejects inbound CONNECT packets whose reserved bit is set (validate_connect_flags), but neither is the server-side obligation.

authoritative_source.summary: The Server MUST validate that the reserved flag (bit 0 of the Connect Flags byte) in the CONNECT packet is set to 0; if it is not 0 it is a Malformed Packet (refer to section 4.13 for error handling).

mapping_review.notes: The generated entry correctly identified the obligation as Server-side (compliance_status not_applicable) and correctly recorded the client codec behavior as a secondary observation: encode leaves bit 0 = 0 and decode rejects a set reserved bit. No correction to the mapping is needed.

evidence.reason: Outbound CONNECT: connect_flags is initialized to 0 and only defined bits (0x02 clean_start, will flag/QoS/retain bits, 0x80/0x40/0xC0 auth) are ORed in. Bit 0 (reserved) is never set, so the client always emits a reserved flag of 0.

Inbound CONNECT: the connect_flags byte is read and passed to validate_connect_flags, which returns Error::IncorrectPacketFormat when (connect_flags & 0x01) != 0. This is defensive client-side decoding of a peer-originated packet, not the server-side Malformed Packet obligation.

test_coverage.notes: The server-side MUST (validate the reserved flag and treat a non-zero value as a Malformed Packet) is not testable in this client crate. The cited codec tests cover only the client-adjacent encode/decode behavior (bit 0 = 0 on encode; decode rejection of a set reserved bit).
